mnemonics.title: LINKED vs ARRAY
mnemonics.content: LINKED: Logical, Individual Nodes, Kinetic (dynamic), Explicit pointers, Dynamic allocation. ARRAY: Address-based, Rigid structure, Requires full/complete tree, Allocation upfront, Yields index math.

mnemonics.title: Double +1/+2 Rule
mnemonics.content: For array indices: Left child = 2i+1 (Double i and Add 1), Right child = 2i+2 (Double i and Add 2). Remember: 'Left is 2i+1 because L comes before R in alphabet (1 < 2)'

mnemonics.title: Parent Formula
mnemonics.content: Parent index = floor((i-1)/2). Remember: 'Subtract 1 before dividing by 2 - like undoing child calculation backwards'

mnemonics.title: Complete Tree Check
mnemonics.content: C-FLOW: Complete trees Fill Levels Without gaps. Array representation works best when tree is Complete, Full, Left-aligned, Occupies consecutive indices, Without missing nodes.

acronyms.usage: Parent index formula: (i-1)/2 with floor operation (Left orientation in array)

acronyms.expansion: Linked for Arbitrary, Array for Dense
acronyms.usage: Choose Linked for arbitrary structures, Array for dense/complete trees

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pyramid of indices: root (0), level 1 (1-2), level 2 (3-6), etc. Draw lines connecting array indices to form the tree structure.
In linked representation, picture dead-end roads branching from each node - left/right pointers either lead to new cities (nodes) or terminate (null)
For incomplete trees in arrays, visualize empty slots as 'ghost nodes' marked with special values (like -∞) between actual elements
Draw linked nodes as islands with two bridges (left/right pointers) connecting them. Missing bridges (null pointers) indicate no connection

analogies.analogy: Like a treasure hunt map - each clue (node) contains directions to two new locations (left/right children). The hunt ends when a clue says 'STOP' (null pointer)
Similar to apartment numbering - ground floor (root) is 0, first floor apartments 1-2, second floor 3-6. Each apartment knows its upstairs neighbors through index math
Like a family tree spreadsheet - each row represents a generation. Children are always at 2i+1 and 2i+2 positions relative to their parent's index i
